exemplar.title: The impact of the educational system on people's waste management habits

detailedExemplarData.title: The impact of the educational system on people's waste management habits

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Uses headings, numbered sections and a contents page to provide structure
Consistent use of appropriate technical terminology
weaknesses: Organisation is uneven (split tables, misplaced figures)
Prose is wordy in parts and formatting errors interrupt flow
Figures lack complete captions and references are inconsistently formatted
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report shows a clear structure that is not sustained throughout (band 2). Technical terminology is used but concision is inconsistent (band 2). The argument is mainly logical but parts are difficult to follow due to formatting and coherence issues (band 2). Overall, this merits 2/3.

strengths: Research question is coherent, focused and clearly defines IV and composite DV
Provides general environmental context linking waste management to climate change
Describes connections between school-based learning and student behaviours
weaknesses: Bundles multiple behaviours into one composite DV, reducing specificity
Lacks local context (e.g., policies in Madrid or The Hague)
Omissions in mechanisms linking educational systems to habits
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student formulates a relevant, coherent research question with sufficient focus (band 5â6 descriptor) but minor clarity issues around the composite DV prevent full marks. The environmental issue is outlined (band 3) but lacks local grounding, and the connection to the research question is described with omissions (band 3). Overall, performance fits the lower half of the 5â6 band, meriting 4/6.

strengths: Method is repeatable with clear procedural steps and equipment list
Stratified sampling strategy is described to match the comparison aim
weaknesses: Sample size is small and no justification is provided (power analysis missing)
Ethical section omits data-protection regulations and deeper risk analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method design is repeatable but insufficient to collect fully relevant data due to self-reports and small sample size (band 3â4). The sampling strategy is described but lacks justification for size and bias control (band 3â4). Ethical and risk considerations are outlined but without comprehensive coverage (band 3â4). These all align with a mid-band mark of 3/6.

strengths: Identifies a few methodological limitations (e.g., vague response options)
weaknesses: No evaluation of conclusion in broader environmental context
Only weaknesses (no strengths) of the method are listed
Improvement suggestions are superficial and lack depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation of the conclusion is minimal, reiterating rather than critically assessing links to the environmental issue (band 1â2). Method evaluation names only some weaknesses (band 1â2). Proposed improvements are superficial (band 1â2). Overall, this performance fits the lower band of 1/6.

strengths: Appropriate charts and tables cover most survey questions
Charts are labelled and referable in the write-up
weaknesses: Data analysis is limited to counts and percentages, omitting comparative statistics or error measures
Raw data contain typographical errors and missing labels
Conclusion does not acknowledge uncertainty or alternative explanations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation is generally appropriate with some omissions (band 3â4), but analysis shows significant omissions and limited statistical rigor (band 1â2). The conclusion interprets trends but lacks critical support and uncertainty discussion (band 3â4). On balance, the major weakness in analysis reduces the overall criterion score to 2/6.

strengths: Proposes a relevant application by embedding waste-management curriculum
Links application to study findings and existing ESS framework
weaknesses: Justification is weak, lacking feasibility or cost-benefit analysis
Evaluation of solution omits factors like teacher training and resource requirements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes one potential solution and provides a weak justification (band 2). The evaluation notes some strengths and weaknesses but omits key feasibility considerations (band 2). This aligns with a mid-band mark of 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Technical terms such as âindependent variableâ, âstratified samplingâ, and âproactivenessâ are used correctly; however, the prose is often wordy and occasionally informal.  The work therefore meets either terminology *or* concision consistently, aligning with band 2.

The discussion reiterates that education influences behaviour but does not critically test how far the conclusion is warranted, nor relate it back to broader environmental significance beyond a passing remark.  This level corresponds to âdescribes how some aspects â¦ relateâ, i.e., band 1-2.

The student states that IB students are âmore proactiveâ and supports this with reference to Figures 1, 4, 5 and specific percentages.  While the sample and analysis are weak, the conclusion does stem from observed trends, so it has *some* validity.  Gaps (no uncertainty, no acknowledgement of alternative explanations) prevent a higher band, fitting 3-4.

Analysis is limited to counting responses and converting a few into percentages cited in the conclusion.  No comparative statistics, measures of central tendency, or error bars are used; several questions are ignored entirely.  These major omissions and the absence of rigour place the work in the 1-2 band, at the lower edge.

The student notes that clearer units and re-sending the survey would improve validity but offers no significant redesign or extension (e.g., triangulation, larger sample, statistical tests).  Recommendations are therefore superficial, matching band 1-2.

A repeatable method is provided: administer the same Google-Form questionnaire to two equal groups (15 + 15).  Detailed questions and basic instructions are included, so another investigator could replicate the procedure.

Nevertheless, the method is insufficient for collecting *relevant* data.  Behavioural frequency is self-reported without validation, the sample size is very small, and confounding factors (culture, socio-economic status) are uncontrolled.  These shortcomings align with the descriptor for 3â4 marks.

Links between the environmental issue (waste generation / climate impact) and the research question are described: the student explains that school-based learning may shape behaviours such as recycling, composting and avoiding single-use plastics.  However, the explanation is brief and omits key mechanisms (e.g., curricula content, peer influence, infrastructure), so several connections are left implicit.  The quality therefore fits the 3â4 band.

The research question explicitly identifies the two educational systems, the target age group, and the behaviour (frequency of specific waste-management habits).  Although the wording is long, it is still precise enough to guide data collection and analysis without ambiguity.  The student maintains a clear causal focus (impact of school system on proactiveness) rather than a vague correlation, so the question is coherent as well as relevant to ESS.

Minor weaknessesâsuch as the inclusion of several habits in one composite DVâslightly reduce clarity, so the strand does not merit the full 6 marks; however, it comfortably exceeds the descriptors for bands 1-4.

The student identifies one strength (early education) and one limitation (economic disparity) of the proposed solution, giving brief explanation.  Several other relevant factors (teacher training, curriculum overload) are omitted, so evaluation is partial rather than complete, fitting band 2.

Headings, numbered sections and a contents page provide recognisable structure, but consistency falters (tables split across pages, figures inserted without captions).  Organisation is therefore present but not sustained, matching band 2.

Only weaknesses are presented (vague response options, time constraints); strengths and numerical limitations are not systematically analysed.  This minimal identification of weaknesses fits the 1-2 band.

The introduction outlines why waste management matters for climate change, quoting EU recycling statistics and summarising how recycling, composting and re-use reduce GHG emissions.  This gives the reader enough background to see that studentsâ habits are environmentally significant.

The discussion remains general and descriptive; there is no deeper exploration of specific local waste problems in Madrid or The Hague, nor of policy frameworks.  Consequently, the context is outlined rather than discussed in depth, matching the middle band.

The student describes the use of stratified sampling (two strata based on school system) and explains that this matches the aim of comparing the groups.  However, there is no justification of why 15 participants per stratum is adequate, nor discussion of selection bias (friends of the researcher).  Thus the work reaches the âdescribesâ level but not the full justification required for 5-6.

The student outlines two ethical pointsâanonymity/confidentiality and informed consentâand indicates how each is addressed.  Risk is judged negligible and briefly justified.  While adequate, the treatment is brief and omits broader issues (data protection laws, potential discomfort).  This fits the outline standard (3-4 marks).

Raw tables and five graphs are supplied that cover most survey questions.  The charts are appropriate (stacked bars and pie charts) and labelled, but some data (e.g., sample sizes on each figure) are missing and one figure is duplicated.  Therefore the work is âappropriate with some omissionsâ, matching band 3-4.

An applicationâembedding waste-management subjects similar to ESS in other curriculaâis described and linked loosely to findings and literature.  Justification is present but weak (no cost-benefit or feasibility analysis).  This matches band 2.

Overall argument flows from question to method, results and conclusion, but frequent digressions, repetition, and formatting issues (broken sentences, misplaced figures) make some sections hard to follow.  This is âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, corresponding to band 2.

annotations.comment: The dependent-variable categories (daily, weekly, monthly, never) are broad and qualitative. Recommend defining numeric cut-offs or using a Likert scale for precision.
Equipment list is clear and concise, making the method repeatable. Good alignment with procedural needs.
The ethical section omits reference to data-protection regulations (e.g., GDPR) and risk of digital survey misuse; include these considerations.
Raw data table contains typographical errors (e.g., "Madrid (public school sy")). Clean and standardize entries before analysis.
Figure 1 lacks axis labels, units, and sample sizes. Add these to ensure readers can interpret the chart without ambiguity.
Figure 3 is presented without a descriptive caption or legend. Include a brief statement of what the chart shows and define any symbols.
The caption duplicates credit information without explaining data insights. Modify it to summarize key findings shown in the figure.
Figure 4 caption replicates the same credit text. Consolidate repetitive information and focus on describing what the panel illustrates.
The conclusion effectively references Figures 1â5 to support claims about proactive behaviors, demonstrating coherence between data and interpretation.
The conclusion omits any discussion of uncertainty, sampling error, or alternative explanations. Add qualifiers or acknowledge limitations.
The title is concise and clearly conveys the independent and dependent variables, providing a coherent entry point for the investigation.
The table of contents is clear but could benefit from dot leaders or right-aligned page numbers to improve reader navigation.
Bundling multiple waste-management habits under one dependent variable reduces focus. Consider separating these behaviors or defining a composite index with clear weighting.
The introduction outlines general climate-change impacts but lacks local context (e.g., waste-management policies in Madrid or The Hague) to ground the research question.
The rationale mentions education but omits policy frameworks or curriculum comparisons. Strengthen the context by citing specific programs influencing waste habits.
The independent variable measurement relies solely on one questionnaire item. Consider triangulating with school records or observations to validate self-reports.
Control variables omit socio-economic status and cultural factors, which may confound comparisons. Justify how these are addressed or add additional controls.
The procedural steps omit instructions on follow-up reminders and data validation, such as time frames for survey completion or response checks.
The survey key references colors but you never apply those to charts. Include a legend or annotate charts to match this coding.
Many questions lack entries in the "Why the question was asked" column. Complete these to demonstrate alignment with the research objectives.
Sampling strategy description calls Google Form a sampling method rather than a data-collection tool. Clarify stratified sampling rationale and process.
The write-up does not justify sending to only 15 respondents per group. Incorporate a power analysis or discuss sampling sufficiency.
Percentages are cited without detailing how they were calculated. Include a brief note on whether rounding or aggregation was applied.
Discussion identifies survey limitations but omits any strengths of the design or broader methodological insights. Balance evaluation accordingly.
The evaluation notes vague response ranges but does not explore how this affected data patterns. Discuss specific impacts on trend interpretation.
The application insight linking IBâs ESS course to proactiveness is well argued, demonstrating potential for curricular transfer.
The solution evaluation mentions economic disparities but lacks discussion of feasibility and resource requirements. Strengthen with cost/benefit analysis.
References show inconsistent formatting and missing details (e.g., publisher location). Apply a single style guide for coherence.
Several web sources lack access dates or DOIs. Ensure each reference includes complete retrieval information for verification.
Some entries in the final bibliography lack author names or publication dates. Confirm and add missing metadata to enhance reliability.
